questions: The library's time ruler renders at 32px height with specific tick styling. Do you want pixel-perfect parity with the existing ruler, or is a clean reimplementation with similar functionality acceptable?

Virtualization: The library uses react-virtualized for the grid. With typical track counts (2-8), native scroll should be fine. Is there a scenario where you'd have 50+ tracks that would require windowed rendering?

The library's resize handles use interact.js which provides features like inertia, snap-to-grid, and minimum-size constraints. Do you need any of these, or is a simple pointer-event resize with just minimum-width clamping sufficient?
Should this be done as one large PR or broken into smaller PRs (e.g., PR1: own types + TimelineCanvas shell, PR2: resize + wiring, PR3: cleanup)?
success_criteria: Zero imports from @xzdarcy/react-timeline-editor or @xzdarcy/timeline-engine remain in the codebase
npm package @xzdarcy/react-timeline-editor is removed from package.json and node_modules
Bundle size decreases by ~200KB+ minified (interact.js + react-virtualized + timeline-engine removed)

All 12 interaction paths pass manual testing: ruler click, cursor drag, left resize, right resize, internal drag, cross-track drag, external file drop, external asset drop, new-track drop, clip split, clip delete, playback sync
tsc --noEmit and npm run build succeed with zero errors
No visual regressions: clips render at correct time positions, ruler ticks align with clip boundaries, cursor tracks playhead correctly
Existing hooks (useClipDrag, useClipResize, useDragCoordinator, useExternalDrop) require minimal or zero changes to their core logic
assumptions: Track count stays under ~20, so native scroll without virtualization is acceptable. Virtualization can be added later if needed.
The library's autoScroll feature (auto-scroll during drag) is handled by useClipDrag's existing scroll logic, not the library.
The `effects` map passed to the library (data.effects) is not meaningfully used by the library at runtime since we use custom renderers — it can be dropped from TimelineCanvas props.
The library's `onChange` callback is only needed for resize events (since movable: false disables move-driven onChange). The custom resize handler will call the same applyTimelineEdit path directly.
The `dragLine` assist line feature (snapping guidelines during drag) is not critical and can be omitted — useClipDrag doesn't use it.

The library's cursor rendering (blue vertical line with draggable top handle) can be simplified to a plain vertical line positioned by time. The drag-on-ruler interaction replaces the cursor drag handle.
